title: Chlorophora excelsa
intro: Chlorophora excelsa, also known as the iroko tree, is a species of deciduous tree native to tropical Africa. The iroko tree has been traditionally used in folk medicine for its various health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antifungal properties, which can aid in the treatment of wounds, infections, and skin conditions. The bioactive constituents of Chlorophora excelsa include flavonoids, terpenoids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made from Chlorophora excelsa are infusions, decoctions, and extracts, which are used to treat a range of health issues, including fever, rheumatism, and digestive problems.
taxonomy: Chlorophora excelsa is a species of tree in the family Moraceae. It is native to tropical Africa and is commonly known as the iroko or African teak. The tree is classified as a member of the order Rosales and is further classified into the family Moraceae and the genus Chlorophora. Within the genus Chlorophora, C. excelsa is a single species and is often referred to as the sole representative of the genus. The classification of C. excelsa is also sometimes referred to as Chlorophora excelsa, although this can be considered a synonym.
uses: Chlorophora excelsa, native to the tropical regions of Africa, has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. The bark of this tree is known to have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which can be used to treat skin infections and wounds. In some African cultures, the tree's leaves and bark are used to treat fever, rheumatism, and other ailments. The root of Chlorophora excelsa is also used to treat digestive issues, such as diarrhea and stomach ulcers. Additionally, the tree's extracts have been studied for their potential antioxidant and anti-cancer properties.
